title: How to dump an energy vampire
intro: Need some advice? In this edition of Dear Life Kit, we're pulling wisdom from the vast Life Kit archives to help you: -Avoid energy vampires -Set firmer boundaries with friends at work -Navigate your own success when others are strugglingHave a question for the Life Kit team? Write us or send a recording to lifekit@npr.org. Questions about money, health, home and family life, staying organized, sticky social situations -- we want them all. We may answer your question in a future episode. Follow us on Instagram: @nprlifekitSign up for our newsletter here.Have an episode idea or feedback you want to share? Email us at lifekit@npr.orgSupport the show and listen to it sponsor-free by signing up for Life Kit+ at plus.npr.org/lifekit

transcript.author: MARIELLE SEGARRA, HOST
transcript.text: I think there's, like, a gnome stealing my jewelry.

transcript.author: MALAKA GHARIB, BYLINE
transcript.text: You better do some kind of trick.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: An ancestral ring, a hoop - one gold hoop earring, a necklace with the Virgin Mary on it have all gone missing.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: I think you need to provide an offering. Yeah.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: What do you think they like?

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: I think you should - can I just say an idea?

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Offer, like, a decoy tray of, like, other jewelry and, like, put it in an obvious place, and so they can be attracted to that decoy jewelry.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: What about those fake gold coins?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: I think they're trying to get your attention that they need some useful things. Like, get a Barbie car or get a little Barbie mansion, you know?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: They're telling you. They're telling you.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I'm like, what do you people want?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...Value things 'cause they need some things of value, I think, is what's happening.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: We're just talking, but I feel like this is very actionable advice.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: This should be the bonus tip, actually.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: OK. Well, hi. It's Marielle Segarra. Welcome to another edition of Dear LIFE KIT, the series where you send us your questions, and we try to dole out some helpful advice. We're actually trying something new today. All of the advice is going to come from us, the LIFE KIT team, with help from the collective wisdom in our archives. And as always, for Dear LIFE KIT reporter Andee Tagle is here. Hi, Andee.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: And we've also got our digital editor, Malaka Gharib, here.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Let's get right into it with our first question. Andee, you want to read that one?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: OK. (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, this is going to sound super petty, and it kind of is. But I'm worried about losing my friends when my life is going better than theirs. One of my friends' parents is having health issues. Another friend is struggling with bills, and another is caring for their grandparents. I've become scared to share things with them because my problems don't feel as big, and I've recently had some positive things happen in my career. We're all in our early 20s, and I know it's not abnormal for everyone to be on different paths and such, but I'm still worried about stoking resentment. What do I do?

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Yeah. I mean, I get this, and it's going to be very common in all of our lives that sometimes when you're up, other people are down and vice versa, right? And also...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: ...Sometimes even within your own life, often, parts of your life are up and parts of your life are down. Like, I have had personal experience with when I got cancer, people - close friends of mine feeling like they shouldn't tell me certain things in their lives. And actually, it wasn't even good stuff happening. It was bad stuff, but stuff that they thought didn't compare to having cancer at 34. So they were like, well, she's going through chemo, so I can't talk to her about my breakup. And I didn't feel that way at all. Like, I was - I remember saying to them...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: It's like pain Olympics. And they're like...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: That's not how I see the world. And I do think fundamentally that we have to be able to be there for the people we love, whether their lives are up or down, that it's just as much of a service to others to show up for them when the good things happen and not hold it against them.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Well, I have a question about that 'cause, like, if you do go through a breakup and then you see that all your friends are getting married or getting with people, yeah, I don't want to hear about your - the fact that you're getting married, right? Like, why is it in that situation that it's kind of normal to, like, not want to hear, oh, gosh, they're getting married? But then, yeah, it's interesting that you're saying that when you were going through cancer and going through this horrible thing, that it was OK for you to hear about other breakups. Is it because the context was totally different?

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I think the context being different helps. And I also had no problem hearing about the good things in people's lives.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: It actually just reminded me that there was good on the other side of what I was going through, like my friends being in Europe, you know, with their kids and sending pictures. I was like, oh, my gosh, I can't wait to go there when I'm done with treatment. But I think, yeah, you want to make sure with your friends that you're also showing up for them and not just asking, how are you? Like, if awful things are happening in their lives, then you want to be present and think about, like, what you're good at and practical ways you could show up, Like, if they're going to a lot of doctors' visits, maybe you're good at being pushy and advocating for someone or maybe you're good at organizing the meal train or maybe you're really good at emotional support. You want to think about the specifics of the situation that they shared with you and how you can show up for them.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: A trick that I found helpful with this is something that I got from the team.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: And it was just ask, in the reverse situation, would you be offended? You know? Like, if something bad was happening to me and something good was happening to my friend...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...Would it hurt my feelings? Would I want to bring my friends down with me? Like, of course not. Like, I would - you know, if someone is in your corner, I found...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...In my personal experience, like, if someone's in my corner, they're in my corner, no matter what. And if someone wants...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...To yuck on my yum, they're going to yuck on my yum no matter how well they're doing. You know what I mean?

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Also it's a good - this is maybe a good litmus test, too. It's like, if she can come to these people, her friends, and, like, she feels comfortable enough to celebrate with them, no matter where they are, and they like, yes, I think reciprocate is important. I think that's, like, a sign that, like, it's a good friendship. But if they're, like - if she doesn't feel comfortable in that space, too, then maybe it's also not a good sign.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Let's go to question two. What do you think?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, how do you deal with people you can't stand, but are forced to interact with on a regular basis? I'm in high school, and there's this person who I have many classes with, share mutual friends with, but she's an energy vampire. She loves to one-up me and has a mean sense of humor. How can I set clearer boundaries or make my interactions with her less painful?

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: OK. So I ought to tell you guys something. I, like, attract energy vampires. I don't know how and why. But they come to me, they dump on me.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: They tell me all their - like, they vent at me, and they talk at me, and then, like, they don't let me - leave me any room to, like, speak. And I think that it's because I'm way too nice. Like, I don't - and I don't have any, like, boundaries with friendships, too. It's like, I'll just, like, keep hanging out with a person because I feel obligated.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: So for this high school student, I spent a lot of time looking through all of our friendship episodes, and I found the perfect advice for you...

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: ...From an interview with the writer Rachel Wilkerson Miller, who wrote the book "The Art Of Showing Up: How To Be There For Yourself And Your People." And her advice was this - break up with a friend like you would break up with a partner. Here's what you might say to this energy vampire. Listen, I appreciate that you want to be friends with me, but I'm just not feeling the connection. I know we're in a lot of classes together, so let's try to be cordial with each other when we see each other. But for now, I'd appreciate if you gave me a little bit of space. I hope you understand.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: That seems, like, maybe easier to say over text.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: I mean, I think that your response, Malaka - I also think it's a mature one.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Maybe a little too mature. Yeah.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. Here's the thing. OK. So my initial thought was I think it's a mature one, and I think it would be very good in an ideal world.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...Is very far from an ideal world, right? I think...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. I think it's very far from an ideal world.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Yeah. So what's - what would you do?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: OK. So my solution - the first thing that came to my mind - it's a weird one. Stick with me.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: But the first episode that came to my head - the first advice that came to my head was my - the episode on how to raise a toddler, on how to talk toddler.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Oh, my God. That is not the connection I thought you were going to make.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: OK. This person can be really - sounds really irritating. Sounds like they're being really illogical, like they're being really nonsensical, not very far away from a toddler. And when you are communicating with a toddler and you want them to change their behavior, you have to be really creative. So sometimes that means you have to, like, out wacky their wacky. Sometimes you have to...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...Like, distract them or give them, like, a hard time limit. Or...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: In the case of, like, an all-out tantrum, like, if this person's being really mean, then what you have to do is, like, mentally disengage, protect your peace, you know, do nothing to fan the flames.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Or I might try to, like, positively reinforce someone else's behavior, you know?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Like, I might, like, go to someone who I actually think is cool and, like, try and talk to them about that thing that I thought...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: But you know what I mean?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Like, I might just, like, turn my back.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: They tell me a mean joke.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: You say something mean about me, Malaka. I know, I can't imagine it ever.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I truly can't imagine it happening, but you say something mean to me, or you one-up me...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: ...And I just literally, like, don't respond to you, like, eyes glaze over and I'm like...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Just, like, don't - you have to laugh at the joke, right? Like, making -keep telling the jokes. You don't have to laugh at the joke.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Or even - like, even if I was just talking to you, I could be like, did you say that you were going to the beach this weekend?

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Like, the distraction thing, I think, could be really helpful.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Sometimes, when people are mean. I feel like it's coming from an insecure place and, like, a - trying to prove you're so cool...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: And so just being hard with them. Like, being a little - like, you can be - there's this meme that I saw that I really love. It's a bunny rabbit, holding a medieval weapon. Like...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: ...It's, like - it has, like, a chain with, like, a ball on the end and a bunch of metal spikes. And it says, soft but not available for mistreatment.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: That's how I feel. That's how I feel like I am.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Like, people feel like they - that's why I attract the energy vampires, I think. It's 'cause I think they can just sort of, like...

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: ...Dump on me. But, like, I think that I have to have a boundary. Like...

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: ...This is the thing about this person. Like, you're very nice to, like, entertain these kind of treatment, but, like, yeah.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: You're soft, but, like, don't mistreat me.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: All right. Let's go on to question three.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, I have a coworker who I absolutely adore, and we're friends outside the workplace as well. She has three young children, and she prioritizes being a good mom over things at work. But when she has to call out for doctor's appointments, sick days or holidays, the job of picking up the slack at work tends to fall on me. Our boss doesn't see the problem, and I realize it's my own fault for taking things on. I have a lot of trouble delegating or asking for help since I often find myself so stressed that it's easier just to do things myself. I'm happily childless myself, and I want my friend and coworker to be able to prioritize her family. I feel terrible for feeling resentful. How do I stop this cycle of stress?

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Quit your job. Just kidding. Just kidding.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: OK. I think this is a really juicy question. And at first glance, it seems like a parent-versus-child-free values type of question, I think. But I think actually, this is a you-versus-you question. Personally, I think that's what it - do you agree? Do you agree?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: It's a you versus the perceived expectations of you, like, the largely unstated expectations of you, letter writer. And I want to just start by saying the people pleaser in me sees the people pleaser in you. Just say. Something I learned recently from our episode on the book "Drained" from a professor named Leah Ruppanner is that sometimes, when it comes to the mental load, sometimes you need to separate out what is required of you, what you're taking on out of obligation and what you actually want to be doing. Like, does it really have to be on you to take on this work bestie's load, you know? Do you have to raise your hand every time? Does anyone have to raise their hand for this work, you know? Are your deadlines real deadlines? These are the things that I'm thinking of. What about you guys?

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: And I think ultimately, like, I know sometimes if we care about our jobs or even if we're diligent type of people, we feel like the work is our responsibility, and if it doesn't all get done, then it's on us. But ultimately, it's the company's responsibility, and it's the company's problem if it's not all getting done.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: And their challenge to figure out.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Right. That's exactly what I said, is, like, it sounds like you need to talk to your boss about this. It sounds like you have tried before and it hasn't been successful. Like, one - it doesn't have to stop at one conversation, right? So, like, it seems like it's time to ask your boss for a meeting, give yourself time to have - to think about what you want to say. And when you do that, data is your friend. Keep your receipts if you haven't already. Arm yourself with information if you feel like you've been doing a lot of the work for a lot of the time and you're OK with that, then maybe it's time for a new role, a new title, more money so that you can feel less resentful.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Let this be your flag - red flag.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah. Or if you're not, then let your boss know, I'm doing way too much and it's time to pare back.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I think that another point here is often in the workplace, people who have kids are given more leeway or more, like, help. And I understand that they do need the support, but that doesn't mean that someone who's child-free should just automatically have to work more than someone...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: ...We've done an episode on how to be child-free, and part of that is just reinforcing that that is a valid choice, and it doesn't make you a better person, a more upstanding member of society or more important because you have kids versus someone else.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Maybe you need to hear that, actually. Maybe the letter writer needs to hear that.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. I had the exact same thing to say. Brittany Luse, the host of It's Been A Minute, came on to Dear LIFE KIT a few months back, and she had this great line. She says, I'm going to give as much as I can lovingly and no more because if you give more than what you can give lovingly, you're either going to - it either leads to conflict, or you end up feeling short-changed. So I think it applies to these friends here.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: It applies to friends. It applies to family. You know, I will - I'll give you as much as I can that I can give lovingly. But as soon as I feel any, like - any sort of bitterness in my mouth, that's the line. That's the line. It doesn't matter if I have kids, if I don't have kids. Like, whatever I can give lovingly.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Once I'm unhappy about it, that's the line to stop. Then you're asking too much.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: Wow. That also applies to the other letter writer with the energy vampire.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Probably applies to all of them.

transcript.author: GHARIB
transcript.text: That's right. Yeah. That's good advice.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: I think so, too. Thanks, Brittany Luse.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: If you have a question for LIFE KIT, dear listener, send it to us. We want to hear your questions about money, about healthcare, about staying organized, exercising, messy breakups, annoying friends, whatever you've got. Email us your question or record yourself asking it and send the file to lifekit@npr.org.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: I'm Marielle Segarra. Thanks for listening.

lines: I think there's, like, a gnome stealing my jewelry.
You better do some kind of trick.
An ancestral ring, a hoop - one gold hoop earring, a necklace with the Virgin Mary on it have all gone missing.
I think you need to provide an offering. Yeah.
What do you think they like?
I think you should - can I just say an idea?
Offer, like, a decoy tray of, like, other jewelry and, like, put it in an obvious place, and so they can be attracted to that decoy jewelry.
What about those fake gold coins?
I think they're trying to get your attention that they need some useful things. Like, get a Barbie car or get a little Barbie mansion, you know?
They're telling you. They're telling you.
I'm like, what do you people want?
...Value things 'cause they need some things of value, I think, is what's happening.
We're just talking, but I feel like this is very actionable advice.
This should be the bonus tip, actually.

OK. Well, hi. It's Marielle Segarra. Welcome to another edition of Dear LIFE KIT, the series where you send us your questions, and we try to dole out some helpful advice. We're actually trying something new today. All of the advice is going to come from us, the LIFE KIT team, with help from the collective wisdom in our archives. And as always, for Dear LIFE KIT reporter Andee Tagle is here. Hi, Andee.
And we've also got our digital editor, Malaka Gharib, here.
Let's get right into it with our first question. Andee, you want to read that one?

OK. (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, this is going to sound super petty, and it kind of is. But I'm worried about losing my friends when my life is going better than theirs. One of my friends' parents is having health issues. Another friend is struggling with bills, and another is caring for their grandparents. I've become scared to share things with them because my problems don't feel as big, and I've recently had some positive things happen in my career. We're all in our early 20s, and I know it's not abnormal for everyone to be on different paths and such, but I'm still worried about stoking resentment. What do I do?
Yeah. I mean, I get this, and it's going to be very common in all of our lives that sometimes when you're up, other people are down and vice versa, right? And also...

...Sometimes even within your own life, often, parts of your life are up and parts of your life are down. Like, I have had personal experience with when I got cancer, people - close friends of mine feeling like they shouldn't tell me certain things in their lives. And actually, it wasn't even good stuff happening. It was bad stuff, but stuff that they thought didn't compare to having cancer at 34. So they were like, well, she's going through chemo, so I can't talk to her about my breakup. And I didn't feel that way at all. Like, I was - I remember saying to them...
It's like pain Olympics. And they're like...

That's not how I see the world. And I do think fundamentally that we have to be able to be there for the people we love, whether their lives are up or down, that it's just as much of a service to others to show up for them when the good things happen and not hold it against them.

Well, I have a question about that 'cause, like, if you do go through a breakup and then you see that all your friends are getting married or getting with people, yeah, I don't want to hear about your - the fact that you're getting married, right? Like, why is it in that situation that it's kind of normal to, like, not want to hear, oh, gosh, they're getting married? But then, yeah, it's interesting that you're saying that when you were going through cancer and going through this horrible thing, that it was OK for you to hear about other breakups. Is it because the context was totally different?
I think the context being different helps. And I also had no problem hearing about the good things in people's lives.

It actually just reminded me that there was good on the other side of what I was going through, like my friends being in Europe, you know, with their kids and sending pictures. I was like, oh, my gosh, I can't wait to go there when I'm done with treatment. But I think, yeah, you want to make sure with your friends that you're also showing up for them and not just asking, how are you? Like, if awful things are happening in their lives, then you want to be present and think about, like, what you're good at and practical ways you could show up, Like, if they're going to a lot of doctors' visits, maybe you're good at being pushy and advocating for someone or maybe you're good at organizing the meal train or maybe you're really good at emotional support. You want to think about the specifics of the situation that they shared with you and how you can show up for them.
A trick that I found helpful with this is something that I got from the team.
And it was just ask, in the reverse situation, would you be offended? You know? Like, if something bad was happening to me and something good was happening to my friend...
...Would it hurt my feelings? Would I want to bring my friends down with me? Like, of course not. Like, I would - you know, if someone is in your corner, I found...
...In my personal experience, like, if someone's in my corner, they're in my corner, no matter what. And if someone wants...
...To yuck on my yum, they're going to yuck on my yum no matter how well they're doing. You know what I mean?

Also it's a good - this is maybe a good litmus test, too. It's like, if she can come to these people, her friends, and, like, she feels comfortable enough to celebrate with them, no matter where they are, and they like, yes, I think reciprocate is important. I think that's, like, a sign that, like, it's a good friendship. But if they're, like - if she doesn't feel comfortable in that space, too, then maybe it's also not a good sign.
Let's go to question two. What do you think?

(Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, how do you deal with people you can't stand, but are forced to interact with on a regular basis? I'm in high school, and there's this person who I have many classes with, share mutual friends with, but she's an energy vampire. She loves to one-up me and has a mean sense of humor. How can I set clearer boundaries or make my interactions with her less painful?
OK. So I ought to tell you guys something. I, like, attract energy vampires. I don't know how and why. But they come to me, they dump on me.

They tell me all their - like, they vent at me, and they talk at me, and then, like, they don't let me - leave me any room to, like, speak. And I think that it's because I'm way too nice. Like, I don't - and I don't have any, like, boundaries with friendships, too. It's like, I'll just, like, keep hanging out with a person because I feel obligated.
So for this high school student, I spent a lot of time looking through all of our friendship episodes, and I found the perfect advice for you...

...From an interview with the writer Rachel Wilkerson Miller, who wrote the book "The Art Of Showing Up: How To Be There For Yourself And Your People." And her advice was this - break up with a friend like you would break up with a partner. Here's what you might say to this energy vampire. Listen, I appreciate that you want to be friends with me, but I'm just not feeling the connection. I know we're in a lot of classes together, so let's try to be cordial with each other when we see each other. But for now, I'd appreciate if you gave me a little bit of space. I hope you understand.
That seems, like, maybe easier to say over text.
I mean, I think that your response, Malaka - I also think it's a mature one.
Maybe a little too mature. Yeah.
Yeah. Here's the thing. OK. So my initial thought was I think it's a mature one, and I think it would be very good in an ideal world.
...Is very far from an ideal world, right? I think...
Yeah. I think it's very far from an ideal world.
Yeah. So what's - what would you do?
OK. So my solution - the first thing that came to my mind - it's a weird one. Stick with me.
But the first episode that came to my head - the first advice that came to my head was my - the episode on how to raise a toddler, on how to talk toddler.
Oh, my God. That is not the connection I thought you were going to make.

OK. This person can be really - sounds really irritating. Sounds like they're being really illogical, like they're being really nonsensical, not very far away from a toddler. And when you are communicating with a toddler and you want them to change their behavior, you have to be really creative. So sometimes that means you have to, like, out wacky their wacky. Sometimes you have to...
...Like, distract them or give them, like, a hard time limit. Or...
In the case of, like, an all-out tantrum, like, if this person's being really mean, then what you have to do is, like, mentally disengage, protect your peace, you know, do nothing to fan the flames.
Or I might try to, like, positively reinforce someone else's behavior, you know?
Like, I might, like, go to someone who I actually think is cool and, like, try and talk to them about that thing that I thought...
But you know what I mean?
Like, I might just, like, turn my back.
They tell me a mean joke.
You say something mean about me, Malaka. I know, I can't imagine it ever.
I truly can't imagine it happening, but you say something mean to me, or you one-up me...
...And I just literally, like, don't respond to you, like, eyes glaze over and I'm like...
Just, like, don't - you have to laugh at the joke, right? Like, making -keep telling the jokes. You don't have to laugh at the joke.
Or even - like, even if I was just talking to you, I could be like, did you say that you were going to the beach this weekend?
Like, the distraction thing, I think, could be really helpful.
Sometimes, when people are mean. I feel like it's coming from an insecure place and, like, a - trying to prove you're so cool...
And so just being hard with them. Like, being a little - like, you can be - there's this meme that I saw that I really love. It's a bunny rabbit, holding a medieval weapon. Like...
...It's, like - it has, like, a chain with, like, a ball on the end and a bunch of metal spikes. And it says, soft but not available for mistreatment.
That's how I feel. That's how I feel like I am.
Like, people feel like they - that's why I attract the energy vampires, I think. It's 'cause I think they can just sort of, like...
...Dump on me. But, like, I think that I have to have a boundary. Like...
...This is the thing about this person. Like, you're very nice to, like, entertain these kind of treatment, but, like, yeah.
You're soft, but, like, don't mistreat me.
All right. Let's go on to question three.

(Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, I have a coworker who I absolutely adore, and we're friends outside the workplace as well. She has three young children, and she prioritizes being a good mom over things at work. But when she has to call out for doctor's appointments, sick days or holidays, the job of picking up the slack at work tends to fall on me. Our boss doesn't see the problem, and I realize it's my own fault for taking things on. I have a lot of trouble delegating or asking for help since I often find myself so stressed that it's easier just to do things myself. I'm happily childless myself, and I want my friend and coworker to be able to prioritize her family. I feel terrible for feeling resentful. How do I stop this cycle of stress?
Quit your job. Just kidding. Just kidding.

OK. I think this is a really juicy question. And at first glance, it seems like a parent-versus-child-free values type of question, I think. But I think actually, this is a you-versus-you question. Personally, I think that's what it - do you agree? Do you agree?

It's a you versus the perceived expectations of you, like, the largely unstated expectations of you, letter writer. And I want to just start by saying the people pleaser in me sees the people pleaser in you. Just say. Something I learned recently from our episode on the book "Drained" from a professor named Leah Ruppanner is that sometimes, when it comes to the mental load, sometimes you need to separate out what is required of you, what you're taking on out of obligation and what you actually want to be doing. Like, does it really have to be on you to take on this work bestie's load, you know? Do you have to raise your hand every time? Does anyone have to raise their hand for this work, you know? Are your deadlines real deadlines? These are the things that I'm thinking of. What about you guys?

And I think ultimately, like, I know sometimes if we care about our jobs or even if we're diligent type of people, we feel like the work is our responsibility, and if it doesn't all get done, then it's on us. But ultimately, it's the company's responsibility, and it's the company's problem if it's not all getting done.
And their challenge to figure out.

Right. That's exactly what I said, is, like, it sounds like you need to talk to your boss about this. It sounds like you have tried before and it hasn't been successful. Like, one - it doesn't have to stop at one conversation, right? So, like, it seems like it's time to ask your boss for a meeting, give yourself time to have - to think about what you want to say. And when you do that, data is your friend. Keep your receipts if you haven't already. Arm yourself with information if you feel like you've been doing a lot of the work for a lot of the time and you're OK with that, then maybe it's time for a new role, a new title, more money so that you can feel less resentful.
Let this be your flag - red flag.
Yeah. Yeah. Or if you're not, then let your boss know, I'm doing way too much and it's time to pare back.

I think that another point here is often in the workplace, people who have kids are given more leeway or more, like, help. And I understand that they do need the support, but that doesn't mean that someone who's child-free should just automatically have to work more than someone...

...We've done an episode on how to be child-free, and part of that is just reinforcing that that is a valid choice, and it doesn't make you a better person, a more upstanding member of society or more important because you have kids versus someone else.
Maybe you need to hear that, actually. Maybe the letter writer needs to hear that.

Yeah. I had the exact same thing to say. Brittany Luse, the host of It's Been A Minute, came on to Dear LIFE KIT a few months back, and she had this great line. She says, I'm going to give as much as I can lovingly and no more because if you give more than what you can give lovingly, you're either going to - it either leads to conflict, or you end up feeling short-changed. So I think it applies to these friends here.

It applies to friends. It applies to family. You know, I will - I'll give you as much as I can that I can give lovingly. But as soon as I feel any, like - any sort of bitterness in my mouth, that's the line. That's the line. It doesn't matter if I have kids, if I don't have kids. Like, whatever I can give lovingly.
Once I'm unhappy about it, that's the line to stop. Then you're asking too much.
Wow. That also applies to the other letter writer with the energy vampire.
Probably applies to all of them.
That's right. Yeah. That's good advice.
I think so, too. Thanks, Brittany Luse.

If you have a question for LIFE KIT, dear listener, send it to us. We want to hear your questions about money, about healthcare, about staying organized, exercising, messy breakups, annoying friends, whatever you've got. Email us your question or record yourself asking it and send the file to lifekit@npr.org.
I'm Marielle Segarra. Thanks for listening.
